audienceSections.normal: In a first meeting, you will use [[grammar:أَنَا + N]]. Use [[grammar:أَنَا]] before a name or role noun to say who you are. Arabic has no present-tense word for ‘am’ in this pattern. In أَنَا طَالِبَةٌ, طَالِبَةٌ is the role. Do not add an Arabic word for ‘am’.
audienceSections.expert: This chapter presents [[grammar:أَنَا + N]] in a first meeting. This is a first-person verbless nominal clause: أَنَا is the subject and the following nominative nominal is the predicate. Natural English supplies ‘am,’ but the Arabic present clause has zero copula.

audienceSections.normal: Use [[grammar:أَنَا]] before a name or role noun to say who you are. Arabic has no present-tense word for ‘am’ in this pattern. In أَنَا طَالِبَةٌ, طَالِبَةٌ is the role. Do not add an Arabic word for ‘am’.
audienceSections.expert: This is a first-person verbless nominal clause: أَنَا is the subject and the following nominative nominal is the predicate. Natural English supplies ‘am,’ but the Arabic present clause has zero copula.

audienceSections.normal: The Arabic lines are in Modern Standard Arabic. The vowel marks show short vowels and case endings, so read every marked vowel instead of guessing from the bare consonants.
audienceSections.expert: The reading uses fully vocalized Modern Standard Arabic. Lexical citation forms follow the established perfect-stem convention for verbs, while the learner text preserves the attested inflected form and case vocalization.

breakdown.normal:
Read each exact arabic line, then check its natural English meaning. Watch for [[grammar:أَنَا + N]] where it appears.

- مَرْحَبًا. — Hello.
- أَهْلًا. — Hello.
- أَنَا لَيْلَى. — I am Layla.
- أَنَا سَامِي. — I am Sami.
- أَنَا طَالِبَةٌ. — I am a female student.
- نَعَمْ، أَنَا طَالِبٌ. — Yes, I am a male student.
breakdown.expert:
These exact reading lines provide the distributional evidence for [[grammar:أَنَا + N]]. The grammar section above analyzes the relevant morphology and syntax.
